description: Phase-1 replication of puzzle_001 (5x5 easy), 4 toolsets meta-off, n=3-4 per cell, with 1-sigma overlap analysis.
motivation: Test whether the n=1 pilot cost-ratios are real or sampling luck - the cheapest insurance before scaling.
formal_results: cost mu+-sigma: none $1.15+-0.14 (n=4); fine $1.71+-0.50 (n=3); medium $1.18+-0.20 (n=4); coarse $0.82+-0.31 (n=3). none/medium/coarse cost CIs overlap at 1 sigma; only fine is reproducibly separated (most expensive). coarse CV=38% (the pilot saw its low end).
results_description: At replication the easy-puzzle "coarse ~2x cheaper" headline disappears into noise; the only robust effect is that fine is reliably the priciest.
conclusions: Refutes the pilot headline on easy puzzles; the granularity cost-signal does not survive replication at low difficulty (foreshadows difficulty-dependence).
